qa_pairs.question: What performance improvement was observed after adding more workers and optimizing the code?
qa_pairs.answer: Doing this additional step gave us a gain of performance of a factor of almost two compared to what the compiler did by itself. The code ran in roughly 10 seconds on a K20 before and now runs in about 6 seconds.

qa_pairs.question: Which OpenACC clause can be used to combine multiple nested loops into a single, flattened loop?
qa_pairs.answer: The `collapse(N)` clause can be used. This clause is applied to a loop directive, and will cause the next N loops to be collapsed into a single, flattened loop.

qa_pairs.question: When is the `collapse(N)` clause particularly useful?
qa_pairs.answer: It is useful if you have many nested loops or when you have really short loops.

qa_pairs.question: What is the purpose of the OpenACC `tile(N,[M,...])` clause?
qa_pairs.answer: The `tile(N,[M,...])` clause will break the next loops into tiles before parallelizing.

qa_pairs.question: Under what circumstances is the `tile(N,[M,...])` clause beneficial?
qa_pairs.answer: It can be useful if your algorithm has high locality because the device can then use data from nearby tiles.

qa_pairs.question: What is the challenge presented in the bonus exercise?
qa_pairs.answer: The `bonus` folder contains a code which solves the Laplace Equation using the Jacobi method. The challenge is to port it to GPU using OpenACC and determine the performance gain.
